1: The conditional covenant structure of Deuteronomy 28 -- if you fully obey, YHWH will set you high above all nations -- is the very structure Paul argues no one can meet in Galatians 3:10 (all who rely on works of the law are under a curse, for it is written: cursed is everyone who does not continue to do everything written in the book of the law). Christ meets the condition that Israel cannot: his perfect obedience secures the blessing of Deuteronomy 28:1 for all who are united to him (Galatians 3:14: so that the blessing given to Abraham might come to the Gentiles through Christ Jesus). The obedience YHWH requires in Deuteronomy is the obedience Christ provides (Romans 5:19: through the obedience of the one man the many will be made righteous).
2: All these blessings will come upon you and overtake you -- the blessings as a pursuing force rather than a distant reward. As a shadow of grace, the overtaking blessings image the NT's depiction of divine grace as something that finds the believer before the believer can reach it: the father runs while the son is still a great way off (Luke 15:20). The blessings of Deuteronomy 28 that would overtake obedient Israel are the blessings Ephesians 1:3 says are already secured: blessed with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ Jesus. In Christ, the blessings have already overtaken his people.
3: Blessed in the city and blessed in the country -- the comprehensive blessing covering all the spaces of human habitation. As a shadow of the new creation, this total blessing in all locations anticipates the universal blessing of the new creation where the curse is fully reversed: the city (the New Jerusalem, Revelation 21:2) and the renewed earth (Romans 8:21: the creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay). The binary city-country blessing of Deuteronomy 28:3 points toward the new creation's comprehensive renewal under Christ the king.
4: The blessed offspring, crops, and livestock -- the fruitfulness of the covenant family in all dimensions of life. As a type pointing to Christ as firstfruits, the blessed offspring points to the NT's first-fruit theology: Christ is the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep (1 Corinthians 15:20), the firstborn from the dead (Colossians 1:18). The blessing of offspring and land-fruitfulness is the covenant promise fulfilled in Christ as the firstborn and in the new-creation fruitfulness he makes possible (John 15:5: whoever abides in me bears much fruit).
5: Your basket and your kneading bowl will be blessed -- the daily-food provision of the covenant community under YHWH's blessing. As a shadow of Christ as the bread of life, the blessed basket and kneading bowl that daily provide food anticipate Jesus's self-identification as the true bread from heaven: I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me will never go hungry (John 6:35). The covenant blessing on the instruments of daily bread is the type of Christ who himself is the daily bread -- he taught his disciples to pray for this bread (Matthew 6:11) while identifying himself as its ultimate source.
6: Blessed when you come in and blessed when you go out -- the blessing protecting the movement of the covenant people, their coming and going. As a shadow of Christ as the door, this verse anticipates Jesus's self-identification: I am the door; if anyone enters through me, he will be saved, and will come in and go out and find pasture (John 10:9). The blessed coming-in and going-out of the covenant community is the type of the safety Christ provides for those who move through him -- their entry and exit are guarded by the divine door.
7: Enemies defeated before you, coming at you from one direction and fleeing seven -- the covenant blessing of military victory as a type of spiritual victory. The NT applies this pattern to the spiritual battle: thanks be to God who always leads us in triumphal procession in Christ (2 Corinthians 2:14). The seven-direction scatter of fleeing enemies is the type of Christ's complete victory over the powers: he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame by triumphing over them in the cross (Colossians 2:15). What Deuteronomy promises for earthly battle Christ accomplishes in the decisive spiritual battle.
8: YHWH sends a blessing on your barns and everything you put your hand to -- the covenant blessing extending to all work and stored provision. As a revelation of God, YHWH is a God who cares about the material fruitfulness of his people's labor, not just their spiritual state. The NT extends this: whatever you do, work at it with all your heart as working for the Lord (Colossians 3:23). The blessing on barns and labor is the covenant prototype of the NT theology of vocation: human work blessed by divine commission and presence.
9: YHWH will establish you as his holy people as he swore -- the covenant people defined by YHWH's holiness given to them. As a type pointing to the church, this verse anticipates 1 Peter 2:9: you are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people belonging to God. The establishment of Israel as a holy people by YHWH's own holiness is the prototype of the NT's understanding of the church: holy not by its own achievement but by Christ's holiness imputed to it (1 Corinthians 1:30; 2 Corinthians 5:21). YHWH established Israel's holiness by covenant oath; he establishes the church's holiness by Christ's atoning sacrifice.
10: All the peoples of the earth will see that you bear the name of YHWH -- the covenant people as YHWH's visible representatives among the nations. As a shadow of the church's global witness, this anticipates the NT's understanding that the church bears Christ's name to all the nations (Acts 11:26: the disciples were first called Christians; Revelation 3:12: I will write on them my new name). The covenant people bearing YHWH's name before the nations is the OT type of the church that bears Christ's name -- the name that is above every name (Philippians 2:9) -- as its identifying mark in the world.
11: YHWH will grant you abundant prosperity -- the fullness of covenant blessing overflowing in every dimension of life. As a shadow of the spiritual blessings in Christ, the material abundance of this verse is the type of the spiritual abundance Ephesians 1:3 announces: God has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ. The abundant covenant prosperity of Deuteronomy is the shadow; the inexhaustible spiritual riches of Christ are the substance (Ephesians 3:8: the unsearchable riches of Christ). In the new creation, the material and spiritual will both be fully realized (Revelation 21:24-26).
12: YHWH opens his storehouse of heaven to send rain in season -- heaven's treasury opened by covenant faithfulness. As a shadow of grace, the opened storehouse of heaven anticipates the NT's language of divine giving: if you who are evil know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father in heaven give good gifts to those who ask him (Matthew 7:11). The metaphor of the opened heavenly treasury, giving rain in season, is the type of the opened heavens that poured out the Spirit at Pentecost (Acts 2:2: suddenly there came from heaven a sound like a mighty rushing wind). YHWH's heavenly storehouse opened for the covenant community is the type of the Spirit poured out on all flesh.
13: YHWH will make you the head, not the tail -- the status reversal of covenant blessing: the lowest becomes the highest. As a type of Christ's exaltation and the church's participation in his status, this verse anticipates Ephesians 1:22 (God placed all things under Christ's feet and appointed him to be head over everything for the church). The head-not-tail promise for obedient Israel is the type of Christ's actual headship over all things, and the NT's promise that those in Christ will reign with him (Revelation 5:10; 2 Timothy 2:12). The covenant status-reversal is eschatological in Christ -- fully realized in the new creation.
14: The condition of the blessings: do not turn aside from any command, to the right or to the left, by going after other gods. As a type pointing to Christ's perfect faithfulness, the condition Israel must meet is the condition only Christ meets. He is the one who does not turn to the right or to the left (Isaiah 50:7: I have set my face like a flint), who refuses the temptations of the wilderness (Matthew 4:1-11), and who is faithful where Israel was unfaithful (Hebrews 3:2: faithful to the one who appointed him, just as Moses was). The conditional blessings of Deuteronomy 28:1-13 are secured by Christ's unconditional faithfulness.
15: But if you do not obey, all these curses will come upon you -- the covenant's conditional reversal. This is the curse-frame that Paul invokes in Galatians 3:10: all who rely on the works of the law are under a curse, for it is written, cursed is everyone who does not continue to do everything written in the book of the law. Paul does not quote the specific curse-list of Deut 28 but its principle -- the comprehensive liability that comes with not continuing to do everything. Christ enters under this liability (Galatians 3:13: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us) so that those who are in him are no longer under the condemnation the law pronounces.
16: Cursed in the city and cursed in the country -- the direct reversal of the blessing of Deuteronomy 28:3. As a shadow of the full weight of the covenant curse, this verse establishes that the curse is as comprehensive as the blessing: no location, no sphere of life, no activity escapes it. This is the curse that Christ absorbed in full: the one who had no home of his own (Matthew 8:20: the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head) died outside the city gate (Hebrews 13:12), taking the curse of the homeless, the outcast, and the country-less into himself.
17: Your basket and your kneading bowl will be cursed -- the direct reversal of the blessing of Deuteronomy 28:5. The instruments of daily bread under the covenant curse mean daily provision fails. As a shadow of the curse of privation, this anticipates the wilderness-starvation experience of judgment that Christ reversed in the feeding of the five thousand (Matthew 14:13-21) -- the abundant provision of bread in the wilderness is the covenant blessing restored through Christ who is himself the bread of life (John 6:35). The cursed basket is the type of the empty table that Christ fills for his people.
18: Cursed offspring and crops -- the reversal of the covenant blessing on fruitfulness. As a shadow of the curse's reach into generational life, this verse establishes that the curse is not merely personal but familial and agricultural -- it touches every dimension of life. The NT understanding of original sin carries a similar comprehensiveness: sin has affected every aspect of human life and creation (Romans 8:20-22: the whole creation was subjected to frustration). Christ's redemption is equally comprehensive: he who became a curse for us (Galatians 3:13) reverses not just individual sin but the whole web of curse that Deuteronomy 28 describes.
19: Cursed when you come in and cursed when you go out -- the direct reversal of the blessing of Deuteronomy 28:6. As a shadow of total insecurity, this verse establishes that the cursed life has no safety anywhere -- no movement is protected, no door provides security. This is the condition Christ entered into: he who came in to human life (the incarnation) and went out (the crucifixion) in full vulnerability, taking the unprotected exposure of the covenant curse upon himself so that those who enter through him might have the protection of the one who is the door (John 10:9).
20: YHWH will send disaster, confusion, and failure in everything you undertake until you are destroyed -- the comprehensive undoing of human endeavor under the curse. As a shadow of the cross, this is the experience of complete failure that Christ took on: on the cross, everything appeared to have failed -- his mission destroyed, his disciples scattered, his claims apparently falsified. But this apparent failure is the very mechanism of the curse's defeat: in entering the experience of utter failure, Christ absorbs the curse so that those united to him inherit not failure but the success of his resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:57: thanks be to God who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ).
21: YHWH will plague you with disease until you are wiped out from the land -- covenant disease as the sign of covenant breach. As a type of the curse Christ absorbed, this verse is the background for Matthew 8:17 which quotes Isaiah 53:4: he took up our infirmities and bore our diseases. Jesus's healing ministry is not merely compassionate miracle-working -- it is the enactment of his mission to bear the covenant curse of disease. Each healing is a preview of the cross's larger work: the absorption of the disease-curse so that his people go free.
22: Tuberculosis, fever, inflammation, scorching heat, drought, blight, and mildew -- the catalogue of physical and agricultural afflictions. As a shadow of the comprehensive nature of the curse, this list establishes that Deuteronomy's curse-curse covers every dimension of physical existence. Christ bears all of this: he was described in Isaiah 53 as one who was familiar with suffering, a man of sorrows, who bore our griefs and carried our sorrows -- the Hebrew of Isaiah 53:4 uses bodily disease terms (choli, makhov) for what Christ carries. The specific afflictions of Deuteronomy 28:22 are part of the bundle of human suffering Christ enters into fully.
23: The sky above you like bronze, the ground beneath you like iron -- the total shutdown of creation's provision. The bronze sky produces no rain; the iron ground produces no crops. As a shadow of the curse's reversal of the created order, this is the opposite of YHWH opening his heavenly storehouse (Deuteronomy 28:12). The creation that should serve and sustain becomes hostile and unyielding. The NT's portrayal of creation groaning under the curse (Romans 8:22) is the extended form of this image; Christ's death is the purchase-price for the creation's liberation (Romans 8:21: the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to decay).
24: Rain of your land turned to dust and powder -- the anti-rain that destroys rather than nourishes. The cursed rain destroys while the blessed rain nourishes: the very form of provision becomes an instrument of judgment. As a shadow of the curse's perversion of blessing, this anticipates the eschatological judgment language where the winepress of wrath (Revelation 14:19-20; Deuteronomy 19:15) produces blood instead of wine. Christ in Gethsemane sweat great drops of blood (Luke 22:44) -- he enters the zone of the curse where what should be life-producing (sweat from labor) becomes the sign of a battle against death itself.
25: Routed before enemies, going out one way and fleeing seven -- the direct reversal of the blessing of Deuteronomy 28:7. The blessed army that chased seven directions now flees in seven; the head becomes the tail. As a shadow of the cross, this reversal is exactly what happened to Jesus's disciples: they scattered when he was arrested (Matthew 26:56: all the disciples deserted him and fled). The curse of covenant defeat fell on the community gathered around Jesus -- and he bore it alone. The resurrection reverses it: the scattered disciples are regathered (John 20:19-21) into a community that will turn the world upside down (Acts 17:6).
26: Dead bodies as food for birds and wild animals, with no one to frighten them away -- the disgrace of unburied corpses, the ultimate desolation of the covenant community. As a shadow of eschatological judgment, this is the language Revelation uses for the aftermath of the final battle (Revelation 19:17-18: an angel cries to all the birds to gather for the great supper of God). But the cross inverts the image: Christ's body was not left unburied (it was placed in a tomb, John 19:38-42) despite hanging in the cursed position -- the burial itself was the dignified end to the curse-bearing, preparing for the resurrection.
27: The boils of Egypt and tumors and festering sores -- the reversal of the Exodus: the plagues that fell on Egypt now fall on covenant-breaking Israel. As a shadow of the Exodus-reversal that Christ re-reverses, this verse establishes that covenant disobedience undoes the Exodus deliverance. Christ is the new and greater Moses who leads a new Exodus (Luke 9:31: they spoke of his departure, his exodus, which he was about to accomplish at Jerusalem), and his death is the Passover that permanently prevents the reversal of the deliverance (Hebrews 9:12: he entered once for all securing an eternal redemption).
28: YHWH will afflict you with madness, blindness, and confusion of mind -- the spiritual-cognitive dimensions of the curse. As a type of the spiritual blindness Christ came to heal, this verse connects to Luke 4:18 (the Spirit of the Lord is upon me... to give sight to the blind) and John 9:39 (for judgment I have come into this world, so that the blind may see). The madness, blindness, and confusion of the covenant curse are both literal diseases and spiritual conditions. Jesus heals both -- physical blindness as a sign that the spiritual blindness of the curse is being overcome (John 9:3).
29: Groping at midday like a blind person in the dark -- total disorientation under the curse. As a shadow of the darkness at the crucifixion, this verse is striking: darkness fell from noon (midday) until three in the afternoon at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:45; Luke 23:44). The midday darkness of the cross is the moment when the one who is the light of the world (John 8:12) enters the deep darkness of the covenant curse -- groping in the dark at midday -- so that those who were blind would receive their sight. The curse of midday darkness falls on Christ so that his people receive the light of the world.
30: Pledged to a woman but another sleeps with her; house built but not lived in; vineyard planted but not enjoyed -- the three exemptions of Deuteronomy 20:5-7 now inverted as curses. Each life-threshold that should mark flourishing instead marks loss. As a shadow of the disciples' experience at the crucifixion, this is what it felt like to those who followed Jesus: we had hoped he was the one who was going to redeem Israel (Luke 24:21). The promised blessings appeared lost. The resurrection is the restoration of what the curse seemed to take: the bride, the house, and the vineyard are all given back in the new creation (Revelation 21:2, 9; John 15:1).
31: Your ox slaughtered before your eyes but you eat none of it; your donkey stolen; your flock given to enemies -- the immediate loss of the covenant community's economic means under the curse. As a shadow of the disciples' loss at the crucifixion, the community gathered around Jesus saw their security and future stripped away: he was seized, executed, their fellowship scattered. The donkey he rode on Palm Sunday, the flock he had gathered -- all appeared lost. The resurrection reversed this: he appeared to them and restored their commission (John 21:15-17).
32: Sons and daughters given to another nation -- your eyes strained with longing for them all day, helpless to do anything. The covenant curse of family separation and the helplessness of watching it happen. As a shadow of the church's experience as exiles, this anticipates 1 Peter 2:11-12 (I urge you as foreigners and exiles, abstain from sinful desires... live such good lives among the pagans). Christ himself experienced the separation of exile in his ministry (he came to his own and they did not receive him, John 1:11) and entered the ultimate separation on the cross (my God, my God, why have you forsaken me, Matthew 27:46).
33: A nation you do not know devours the produce of your soil and all your labor -- the curse of dispossession by a foreign power. As a shadow of the church as exiles and strangers whose labor belongs to the kingdom not the world, this anticipates 1 Peter 2:11 (strangers and exiles) and Philippians 3:20 (our citizenship is in heaven). The dispossession of the covenant people by foreign nations is also the historical context for Christ's birth: Israel under Roman occupation, its produce and labor taxed by a nation it did not choose (Luke 2:1). Christ enters the curse of occupation that his own people were experiencing.
34: Until you are driven mad by what you see -- the escalating horror of the curse reaching the level of psychological breakdown. As a shadow of the cross, this is the extremity of anguish: Paul's language of Christ becoming a curse for us (Galatians 3:13) suggests the full weight of the curse-experience -- not just legal substitution but personal entering into what curse actually feels like. The disciples watching the crucifixion were driven to near-madness by what they saw: Mary Magdalene weeping outside the tomb (John 20:11), the Emmaus disciples reduced to hopelessness (Luke 24:17-21). The cross drives the curse to its extremity and through it to resurrection.
35: Incurable boils from the sole of your foot to the top of your head -- total bodily affliction with no remedy. As a shadow of Christ bearing total bodily suffering, this verse connects to the portrait of the suffering servant whose appearance was disfigured beyond that of any human being (Isaiah 52:14) and who bore our diseases (Isaiah 53:4). The total-body affliction from foot to head is the type of Christ's comprehensive physical suffering at the crucifixion: the crown of thorns (head), the scourging (back and body), the nail wounds (hands and feet), the spear thrust (side). Every dimension of bodily suffering is represented.
36: YHWH will drive you and the king you set over you to a nation unknown to you or your ancestors -- the exile of king and people together. As a type of Christ being driven out, this verse anticipates the moment when Jesus was handed over to Pontius Pilate (a foreign ruler, a nation Israel had not chosen) and crucified outside Jerusalem's walls. The king driven into exile by YHWH's judgment is the very situation Jesus enters -- the Davidic king expelled, rejected, handed over to foreign power -- so that the exile falls on him rather than on his people. The scattering of Israel's last king to a foreign nation is the shadow of Christ bearing the exile of YHWH's judgment alone (John 12:31: the ruler of this world will be driven out).
37: You will become an object of ridicule and mockery among all the nations -- the shame-curse of public humiliation. As a direct type of the crucifixion, this verse is fulfilled in the mocking of Jesus: the soldiers mocked him (Matthew 27:27-31), the passersby derided him (Matthew 27:39), the chief priests scoffed (Matthew 27:41-43). The nations that mocked him included Rome (the soldiers), Israel (the religious leaders), and the watching crowd. Hebrews applies this shame-motif directly: let us run with endurance, looking to Jesus who for the joy set before him endured the cross, despising the shame (Hebrews 12:2). The shame-curse of Deuteronomy 28:37 is the shame Christ bore at Calvary.
38: You will plant much seed but harvest little because locusts will eat it -- the curse of failed agricultural labor. As a shadow of the futility of human effort under the curse, this verse connects to the NT's themes of seed and harvest. Jesus's parable of the sower (Matthew 13:1-23) shows seed falling in conditions that prevent harvest -- the various impediments to fruitfulness that characterize the fallen world. The curse of failed harvest is reversed in Christ who is himself the seed that falls into the ground and dies to produce a great harvest (John 12:24): I tell you the truth, unless a kernel of wheat falls into the ground and dies, it remains only a single seed; but if it dies, it produces many seeds.
39: Vine planted and tended but no wine drunk, worms eating the grapes -- the curse on the vineyard. As a shadow of Christ as the true vine, this verse is the negative background for John 15:1: I am the true vine, and my Father is the gardener. The cursed vineyard that produces grapes only for worms is the type of Israel's failed fruitfulness (Isaiah 5:1-7: YHWH looked for a crop of good grapes but found only bad fruit). Christ comes as the vine that does produce fruit -- he himself is the wine of the new covenant (Matthew 26:27-29), and those who abide in him bear fruit abundantly (John 15:5).
40: Olive trees throughout the land but no oil to use, olives dropping off -- the curse on olive production. As a shadow of the reversal of the Spirit's anointing, olive oil is the primary biblical image of the Spirit's anointing (the Hebrew mashiach, Messiah, means anointed with oil). The cursed olive trees that drop their fruit without producing oil are the type of the absence of the Spirit under judgment. Christ as the anointed one (the Messiah) receives the fullness of the Spirit (John 3:34: God gives the Spirit without limit) and pours him out on his people (Acts 2:33). The curse of the dropping olive trees is reversed at Pentecost.
41: Sons and daughters but you will not keep them -- they will go into captivity. The curse of losing children to enemy captivity extends the family-loss theme of Deuteronomy 28:32. As a shadow of the church's experience, this connects to the NT's awareness that following Christ can separate families: I came not to bring peace but a sword, to set a man against his father (Matthew 10:34-35). Christ himself entered the ultimate loss -- handed over by his own people (John 19:11), deserted by his disciples (Matthew 26:56) -- and his resurrection is YHWH's restoration of what the curse took away.
42: Locusts will take over all your trees and crops -- the comprehensive agricultural devastation as a curse metaphor. As a shadow of spiritual devastation under judgment, this connects to Joel's locust-plague (Joel 1-2) which is the OT type of eschatological devastation (Revelation develops it as the fifth trumpet's locusts, Revelation 9:1-11). The restoration from locust devastation that Joel promises (Joel 2:25: I will restore to you the years that the swarming locust has eaten) is the promise Christ fulfills -- the wholesale restoration of what the curse has devoured.
43: The foreigner among you will rise higher and higher while you sink lower and lower -- the role reversal under the curse. As a revelation of God, YHWH's judgment inverts the expected social order: the insider becomes the outsider, the outsider the insider. The NT extends this with consistent role reversals: the first shall be last and the last first (Matthew 19:30), the tax collectors and prostitutes entering the kingdom before the religious leaders (Matthew 21:31). Christ is himself the great role reversal: the one who was highest became lowest (Philippians 2:8) so that those who are lowest might be raised (Philippians 2:10-11).
44: The foreigner will lend to you; you will not lend to him -- the reversal of Deuteronomy 28:12's promised lending to all nations. The debtor-status under the curse is the inversion of the creditor-status under the blessing. As a shadow of the debt metaphor in the gospel, this verse anticipates Paul's language: you are not your own; you were bought at a price (1 Corinthians 6:19-20). The debt-status that the covenant curse produces is the condition Christ enters to pay off: at the cross he pays the debt that humanity cannot (Colossians 2:14: he canceled the record of debt that stood against us).
45: All these curses will come upon you, pursue and overtake you until you are destroyed -- the totalizing sweep of the covenant curse for those who did not obey. As the summary of the curse section, this verse is the explicit target of Galatians 3:10-13. Paul's argument is precise: the law curses those who do not do everything it requires; Christ becomes the curse (Galatians 3:13); therefore those who are in Christ are not destroyed by the overtaking curses of Deuteronomy 28:45. The pursuing, overtaking curse meets its limit at the cross, where Christ absorbs it and rises free from it, leading captivity captive (Ephesians 4:8).
46: They will be a sign and a wonder against you and your descendants forever -- the curse as lasting testimony. As a shadow of the cross as the lasting sign, this verse anticipates the cross as the permanent sign in history: we preach Christ crucified, a stumbling block to Jews and foolishness to Gentiles, but to those who are called, the power of God (1 Corinthians 1:23-24). The curse that should have been Israel's permanent sign of shame becomes, in Christ, the permanent sign of God's power and wisdom. The cross is the sign and wonder forever -- not of curse but of redemption.
47: Because you did not serve YHWH your God joyfully and gladly in the time of prosperity -- the root cause of the curse is joyless service. As a revelation of God's character, this verse is remarkable: YHWH does not merely require obedience but joyful obedience. The source of the curse is not technical law-breaking alone but the heart-condition of loveless service. The NT development: God loves a cheerful giver (2 Corinthians 9:7); the father in the parable wanted the elder son to celebrate joyfully (Luke 15:32). Christ models joyful service: who for the joy set before him endured the cross (Hebrews 12:2). His joyful willing sacrifice is the contrast to Israel's joyless compelled service.
48: You will serve your enemies in hunger, thirst, nakedness, and dire poverty -- the iron yoke of enemies replacing the covenantal rest. As a direct type of Christ's yoke vs. the curse's yoke, this verse is the background for Matthew 11:29-30: take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. For my yoke is easy and my burden is light. The iron yoke of Deuteronomy 28:48 is what Christ replaces: the enemies' iron yoke of hunger, thirst, nakedness, and poverty is the curse; Christ's easy yoke is the gospel's gift to those freed from it by his bearing of the curse (Galatians 3:13).
49: A nation from far away, from the ends of the earth, swooping like an eagle -- the Babylon-Rome prophecy of invading power. As a direct historical fulfillment, Jesus applies this to Rome's coming destruction of Jerusalem in 70 CE: when you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, know that its desolation is near (Luke 21:20); wherever the corpse is, there the vultures will gather (Matthew 24:28 -- the eagle-nation is fulfilled in the Roman eagle standards). Jesus's death was intended to prevent this (John 11:50: better that one man die for the people), but the nation rejected him and the judgment came as prophesied.
50: A fierce-looking nation without respect for the old or pity for the young -- the mercilessness of the invading enemy as a sign of YHWH's judgment. As a revelation of God's justice, the mercilessness of the invader is the instrument of YHWH's curse on covenant-breaking. The NT development: those who show no mercy receive no mercy (James 2:13). Christ, who showed mercy to old and young alike (he healed the elderly, Matthew 8:14; raised the widow's son, Luke 7:11-15; received children, Mark 10:14), stands in total contrast to the merciless curse-nation. His mercy is what prevents the execution of Deuteronomy 28:50's terror on those who come to him.
51: They devour the young of your livestock and the crops of your land until you are destroyed -- the relentless consumption by the enemy until nothing remains. As a shadow of the total devastation of judgment, this verse is the economic dimension of what 70 CE inflicted: Josephus records the stripping of Judea's agricultural productivity during the Roman siege. The response of Christ is the covenant of abundance: I have come that they may have life and have it abundantly (John 10:10) -- the abundant life Christ gives is the covenant blessing that the curse of Deuteronomy 28:51 would have taken away.
52: Siege of all your cities, high fortified walls you trusted falling down -- the failure of all human defenses under the curse. As a shadow of the inadequacy of human security, this verse anticipates the NT's critique of trust in earthly protection: do not be anxious about your life (Matthew 6:25); do not store up treasure on earth where moth and rust destroy and where thieves break in and steal (Matthew 6:19). The fortified cities that could not save Israel are the type of all human security-systems that fail. Christ alone is the strong tower: the name of YHWH is a fortified tower; the righteous run to it and are safe (Proverbs 18:10) -- the name of YHWH is now the name of Christ (Acts 4:12).
53: In the desperation of the siege, eating the flesh of your own children -- the extremity of the curse beyond all human horror. As a direct historical prophecy, this was fulfilled in 70 CE: Josephus (Jewish War 6.201-213) records a woman who killed and ate her infant son during the Roman siege. Jesus wept over Jerusalem specifically because he saw this coming: if you had known what would bring you peace (Luke 19:42). His death was intended to prevent this, but the nation's rejection of him led to the curse falling in its most terrible form. The horror of Deuteronomy 28:53 is what the cross was meant to absorb.
54: The most sensitive man becoming cruel and stingy with his brother and his wife and his surviving children -- the curse that destroys even the bonds of natural affection. As a shadow of the dehumanizing power of the curse, this verse establishes that judgment reaches into the character of the covenant people, perverting what is most human in them. The NT application: the love of many will grow cold (Matthew 24:12) as tribulation comes. Christ is the one who maintains love to the end (John 13:1: having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end) even as the curse destroys natural affection in those under judgment.
55: Refusing to share even the children's flesh he is eating -- the total inversion of covenant hospitality and generosity. The sharing of food as the primal covenant act (the table fellowship) is inverted by the curse into its opposite. As a shadow of the reversal Christ accomplishes, the hoarding of food in extremis is the type of the scarcity-mentality the curse produces. Christ's abundant feeding miracles reverse this: he takes the five loaves and two fish (scarcity) and makes them enough for 5000 with 12 baskets left over (Matthew 14:13-21). The curse's scarcity is reversed by the gospel's abundance.
56: The most delicate and sensitive woman -- so refined she would not place her foot on the ground -- will begrudge everything in the siege's desperation. The most refined and protected, defined by her distance from hardship, is brought by the curse to the worst human action. As a revelation of God, this verse establishes that the curse has no respect for privilege: wealth, culture, and refinement cannot shield from the consequences of covenant-breaking. The NT parallel: do not be deceived, God is not mocked -- whatever a person sows, that will he also reap (Galatians 6:7). The curse is impartial in its devastation.
57: Even the afterbirth and the children she bears she will secretly eat -- the curse reaching its most extreme and nightmarish form. As a sign of total covenant dissolution, this verse marks the complete inversion of the covenant community: the community that was to be a light to the nations (Isaiah 49:6) consumes its own future in desperation. The extremity of the curse is what makes the gospel's declaration of freedom so urgent: the son of man came to seek and save the lost (Luke 19:10) -- those being destroyed by the curse are precisely those he came to save.
58: If you do not carefully follow all the words of this law written in this book and do not revere this glorious and awesome name -- the condition: keeping all the words written in the book. As a direct pointer to Galatians 3:10 (cursed is everyone who does not continue to do everything written in the book of the law), this verse is what Paul has in mind when he argues that the law curses those who do not keep it in totality. The written law in its totality is the standard; no one meets it (Romans 3:20); but Christ is the fulfillment of every written word (Matthew 5:17: I did not come to abolish the law but to fulfill it).
59: YHWH will send extraordinary disasters, severe and prolonged plagues -- the supernatural escalation of the curse. As a shadow of eschatological judgment, the extraordinary disasters and prolonged plagues of the covenant curse are the prototype of the bowl-judgments of Revelation (Deuteronomy 15-16), which are themselves modeled on the plagues of Egypt. The covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28 are the OT template for the eschatological plagues that fall on covenant-breakers at the end of history. Christ's bearing of the covenant curse at the cross is the prevention of these eschatological plagues for those in him (John 5:24: whoever hears my word has passed from death to life).
60: He will bring back all the diseases of Egypt that you dreaded -- the Exodus reversed: what was done to Egypt is now done to Israel. As a shadow of the ultimate covenant failure, the return to Egypt-condition is the undoing of everything the Exodus accomplished. Christ is the new and greater Moses who leads a new Exodus (Luke 9:31) that cannot be reversed: he obtained eternal redemption (Hebrews 9:12), not a provisional redemption that can be forfeited by disobedience. The irrevocable Exodus Christ accomplishes at the cross is the antidote to the disease-return of Deuteronomy 28:60.
61: Every kind of sickness and disaster not recorded in this book -- the open-ended catalogue: the curse is not limited to what is written but encompasses everything that opposes human flourishing. As a revelation of God's justice, Deuteronomy 28:61 establishes that the curse covers all sin's consequences, not just the enumerated ones. Paul's sweeping statement in Romans 6:23 (the wages of sin is death) is the NT equivalent: death in all its forms is sin's consequence, written or unwritten. Christ's redemption is equally comprehensive: through one man's obedience the many will be made righteous (Romans 5:19) -- covering every form of the curse's effect.
62: You who were as numerous as the stars will be left few in number -- the reversal of the Abrahamic covenant promise (Genesis 15:5: your offspring will be as numerous as the stars). The curse undoes the multiplication-blessing all the way back to Abraham. As a shadow of the cross's paradoxical fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise, this verse is the darkest reversal -- the stars becoming few. But Paul argues in Galatians 3:16 that the Abrahamic seed-promise is fulfilled in Christ (one seed, not many seeds). Through one person's death, the many become the true stars (Daniel 12:3: those who are wise will shine like the stars forever).
63: Just as YHWH delighted in making you prosper, so he will delight in bringing you to ruin and destruction -- the terrible language of YHWH delighting in judgment. As a revelation of God's justice, this verse is one of the most sobering in the OT: YHWH's delight in blessing can become delight in judgment for the persistently covenant-breaking people. The resolution in Christ is Isaiah 53:10: it pleased YHWH to crush him, to cause him to suffer. The Father's delight-in-judgment is channeled entirely onto the Son at the cross; those in Christ encounter not YHWH's delight-in-destruction but his delight-in-blessing (Zephaniah 3:17: he delights in you with singing).
64: YHWH will scatter you among all nations from one end of the earth to the other -- the diaspora-curse. As a shadow of Christ gathering the scattered, this verse anticipates the historical diaspora that followed 70 CE. But Jesus inverts the scatter: I have other sheep that are not of this sheep pen -- I must bring them also (John 10:16); the high priest prophesied that Jesus would die for the Jewish nation and not only for that nation but also to gather into one the scattered children of God (John 11:52). The curse of worldwide scatter is reversed by Christ who gathers scattered humanity into one body (Ephesians 2:14-16).
65: Among those nations you will find no rest -- no resting place for the sole of your foot -- a restless, anxious heart. As a direct type of what Christ resolves, this verse is the background for Matthew 11:28: come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. The restlessness of the curse -- no resting place, anxious heart -- is exactly the condition Christ addresses when he offers rest. The sole of the foot finding no rest (Deuteronomy 28:65) is inverted in Christ who sets the feet of his people on the rock (Psalms 40:2) and offers true rest to those who have wandered under the curse.
66: Your life will hang in the balance -- you will live in constant dread, day and night, never sure of your survival. As a shadow of Christ's Gethsemane experience, the life hanging in the balance and the dread that has no resolution describes what Christ entered voluntarily: now my heart is troubled, and what shall I say? Father, save me from this hour? No, it was for this very reason I came to this hour (John 12:27). Christ takes the life-hanging-in-the-balance of Deuteronomy 28:66 into himself -- his life literally hangs on a cross -- so that his people might have certainty rather than dread (John 10:28: no one can snatch them from my hand).
67: In the morning you will say if only it were evening; in the evening if only it were morning -- the curse of unrelieved dread in which no time-period brings relief. As a shadow of the resurrection's reversal, this verse describes existence under the curse as a cycle of despair where neither morning nor evening brings hope. The resurrection is the morning that ends all wishing it were otherwise: weeping may last for the night, but joy comes in the morning (Psalms 30:5). Christ's resurrection is the first morning that is not followed by the curse's return: the first day of the week, while it was still dark, and the stone rolled away (John 20:1) -- the dread of Deuteronomy 28:67 ends at the empty tomb.
68: YHWH will send you back to Egypt in ships along a route I said you would never travel again -- the ultimate curse: the Exodus reversed. Egypt-return is the undoing of everything the covenant narrative accomplished: bondage, not freedom; slavery, not inheritance. As a shadow of the irreversible Exodus Christ accomplishes, this verse establishes the worst possible covenant outcome. Hebrews 9:12 uses the decisive word: he entered once for all into the holy place, securing eternal redemption. The once-for-all nature of Christ's redemption is the antidote to the Egypt-return curse: there is no going back, because the price has been paid permanently. Those in Christ cannot be sent back to Egypt.
